entities.name: anumāna
entities.cite: The blind man's hand does give him real information — touch is a genuine pramana, as inference is

entities.name: pratyakṣa
entities.cite: touch is a genuine pramana, as inference is

entities.name: vinipāta
entities.cite: Vinipata, the headlong fall, is then na durlabha — 'not hard to obtain'

entities.name: saraṇa-jñāna
entities.cite: one who relies chiefly on inference does not find his fall hard to come by

entities.name: viṣame pathi
entities.cite: The failure comes from the combination the verse specifies: visame pathi, on broken ground

entities.name: sūkṣma-jñāna
entities.cite: on even ground, walking slowly, it suffices

axioms.name: pramana
axioms.cite: touch is a genuine pramana, as inference is

axioms.name: pramana
axioms.cite: touch is a genuine pramana, as inference is

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: The failure comes from the combination the verse specifies: visame pathi, on broken ground

axioms.name: serial_slow
axioms.cite: Inference fails not because it delivers nothing but because it is local, serial and slow

axioms.name: na_durlabha
axioms.cite: Vinipata, the headlong fall, is then na durlabha — 'not hard to obtain'

denials.name: pramana
denials.cite: The blind man's hand does give him real information — touch is a genuine pramana, as inference is

denials.name: vivartate
denials.cite: Inference fails not because it delivers nothing but because it is local, serial and slow, while the terrain (1.32-33: powers indexed to circumstance, defeaters unsurveyed) is uneven and the reasoner in practice moves at speed

claims.name: pramana
claims.cite: one who relies chiefly on inference

claims.name: asraya
claims.cite: Like a blind man running on rough ground

claims.name: na_durlabha
claims.cite: does not find his fall hard to come by

rejected_readings.label: anumana_not_pramana
rejected_readings.rendering: Inference is not a valid means of knowledge and should be completely rejected.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary explicitly states 'touch is a genuine pramana, as inference is', establishing anumana's legitimacy. Treating it as an invalid pramana contradicts the axiom that establishes anumana as a power.

rejected_readings.label: anumana_always_fails
rejected_readings.rendering: Inference necessarily and always produces headlong falls.
rejected_readings.why: The verse's conditional structure (combination of rough ground AND speed) contradicts a universal claim of failure. The commentary says 'on even ground, walking slowly, it suffices', showing anumana succeeds in favorable circumstances.

rejected_readings.label: pratyaksha_fails_on_rough_ground
rejected_readings.rendering: Direct perception fails on difficult terrain, just as inference does.
rejected_readings.why: The verse isolates anumana's failure; touch/perception is introduced as a genuine pramana that succeeds. The commentary emphasizes that the problem is anumana's serial nature combined with rough terrain, not rough terrain itself.

notes: The verse formalizes the conditional nature of anumana's epistemic failure: not that it is an invalid pramana, but that it cannot sustain reliable knowledge when operating under compounded adverse conditions (rough ground + speed). The six sorts accommodate this: anumana and pratyaksha as powers, vinipata as a manifestation, the conditions as properties.
